title: CARTE: Pretraining and Transfer for Tabular Learning
authors: Myung Jun Kim, Léo Grinsztajn, Gaël Varoquaux (SODA Team, Inria Saclay; Probabl.ai, France)
tokenization_method: Graphlet tokenization: each table ROW is converted into a star-like graph (graphlet). A center/readout node represents the row; one leaf node per non-missing column carries the cell VALUE, and the EDGE from center to that leaf carries the COLUMN NAME. There is no fixed vocabulary of feature slots — every node and edge feature is initialized by a LANGUAGE MODEL (FastText, 300-dim) applied to the raw string of the cell value and the raw string of the column name. Categorical/string values and column names are embedded directly by FastText. Numerical values are encoded as the SCALAR-times-EDGE product: a numerical leaf node's feature is value * (FastText embedding of its column name) (e.g. node for H-index 239 = 239 * E_Hindex). The center node is initialized as the mean of its leaflets. A graph-attentional Transformer then contextualizes each entry against its column name and neighboring entries. Because tokens are strings (open vocabulary) rather than fixed column indices, tables with different/renamed/reordered columns map into ONE shared graph domain with no schema or entity matching.
feature_types_handled: numerical, categorical, string (free-text entries), and missing — mixed, natively in one graph. Missing columns are simply DROPPED from the graphlet (a 10-column row with 1 missing -> 9 leaf nodes), so no imputation token is needed. No native time-varying/temporal axis: each row is a static, unordered bag of column-value pairs; temporal features would have to be hand-flattened into columns. Particular strength is STRING entries (high-cardinality categoricals, entity names) which most tabular models ignore or one-hot/target-encode.
token_granularity: per-(value, column-name) pair. One token = one leaf NODE (the value embedding) plus its incident EDGE (the column-name embedding), fused by element-wise product inside attention. This is a field-value-pair granularity: distinct from FT-Transformer's per-feature token (which has no string column-name semantics) and from TabPFN's per-cell token (which has no edge/relation feature). The column name is a first-class, semantically-embedded relation, not a positional index.
numerical_strategy: raw scalar multiplied into the FastText embedding of its column name (value * E_column), after a global Yeo-Johnson power transform applied per column at preprocessing for fine-tuning stability. There is NO piecewise-linear/periodic/quantile-bin numerical embedding — the numeric expressiveness comes only from scalar*column-name-vector plus the attention context. The paper's own conclusion lists 'refining numerical representations with the ideas of Gorishniy et al. (2022)' as future work, i.e. it explicitly concedes its numerical encoding is weaker than dedicated PLR/periodic embeddings.
categorical_strategy: the category STRING itself is embedded by FastText (open vocabulary). No per-category learned lookup table, no one-hot, no target encoding. Semantically similar strings ('North America' vs 'Northern America', 'London' vs 'Londres') land near each other because FastText is sub-word/semantic, so typos and naming variants are partly resolved for free.
high_cardinality_handling: this is CARTE's core advantage. High-cardinality categorical/string columns are NOT one-hot or target-encoded; each distinct string is mapped through FastText, so cardinality does not inflate parameters and unseen categories at test time still get a meaningful vector. The authors show their benchmark is deliberately high-cardinality (fraction of columns with |C|>10 is 0.625 vs 0.043 in the TabLLM benchmark) and CARTE wins most there. Ablation (Fig 5) shows CARTE is robust to entity-name variants WITHOUT entity matching, whereas KEN embeddings require manual matching to YAGO to help.
temporal_handling: none. No patching, recurrence, or channel-as-token. Each row is treated as a static unordered set of (column,value) pairs; the graph has no time axis. Time-varying features must be pre-flattened into static columns.
scales_to_many_features: Weak/unproven for the topic's 70+-feature, many-row setting. Sequence length per row = number of non-missing columns + 1 center node; attention is masked to the star graph so a single layer is O(k) per row in neighbors, but the published benchmark and pretraining are SMALL-graph / SMALL-sample: pretraining graphlets cap 1-hop relations at 100 and 2-hop at 10, with a median of ~15 1-hop relations (i.e. tables tuned to roughly that many columns); the learning curves and transfer experiments run only train sizes 32-2048. Fine-tuning uses just ONE attention layer (k=1) to avoid over-smoothing, which limits multi-hop feature-interaction modeling. There is no experiment at 70+ columns and no large-n study; the conclusion lists 'optimizing for larger train-sizes' as future work. Compute cost is high and grows with n (Table 2: ~315s learning at n=512 vs ~1s for CatBoost).
downstream_model: CARTE's own graph-attentional Transformer is the model. Pretrained backbone (initial node+edge layers + Aggregation&Readout layer) is reused; a linear head is attached for the task. Fine-tuned per dataset with a bagging ensemble over different train/validation early-stopping splits. For transfer it is fine-tuned jointly on target+source graphs; for multi-table it builds pairwise (target,source_i) learners and ensembles them with a self-supervised softmax weighting.
pretraining_objective: self-supervised CONTRASTIVE learning (SimCLR/InfoNCE, Chen 2020 + Oord 2018) on the YAGO3 knowledge base (~18.1M triplets, ~6.3M entities, built from Wikidata). Each YAGO entity's 2-hop subgraph becomes a graphlet (capped 100 one-hop / 10 two-hop relations); the positive pair is the graphlet and an edge-TRUNCATED copy (randomly drop 30-70% of edges); all other graphlets in the batch are negatives; loss is cosine similarity of the readout vectors fed to InfoNCE. 12 layers x 12 heads, hidden dim 300, 9.3M params, 1M steps (~40 epochs), AdamW + cosine schedule (lr 5e-6 -> 1e-4), batch 256 (128 entities + truncated positives), trained on NVIDIA V100 GPUs. Crucially the prior is REAL background knowledge (entities/relations), not synthetic SCM data as in TabPFN.
key_results: On 51 real datasets (40 regression + 11 classification), CARTE attains the best average normalized score and best critical-difference rank against 42 baselines (CatBoost, XGBoost, HistGB, RandomForest, MLP, ResNet, Ridge/Logistic, TabVec+skrub, and S-LLM e5-small row-serialization) across train sizes 32-2048, with the largest edge in the low-sample regime and on string-heavy/high-cardinality tables. Robust to missing values (Table 1: at n=64, 30% features dropped, CARTE loses 38.4% vs CatBoost 53.3%; higher normalized score throughout). Entity matching NOT needed (Fig 5). On the 9 TabLLM datasets (mostly numerical / low cardinality) TabPFN ranks first and CARTE second, ahead of XGBoost and TabLLM — i.e. CARTE's advantage narrows when there are few strings. Multi-table: CARTE gives CONSISTENT gains from joint learning WITHOUT manual column matching, and the worst-case improves as more (up to 3) source tables are added; CatBoost needs manual schema matching to benefit.
baselines_compared: CatBoost, XGBoost, HistGradientBoosting, RandomForest, MLP, ResNet, Ridge, Logistic Regression, skrub TableVectorizer + tree (TabVec-XGB/RF, Gamma-Poisson high-cardinality encoder), S-LLM (e5-small-v2 row-serialization + XGB, with Concat-Numerical and Embed-Numerical variants), KEN knowledge-graph embeddings, TabPFN, TabLLM. 42 baseline configurations in total.
transferable_idea: Embed the COLUMN NAME (the relation) with a language model and fuse it with the cell value, so a token is a (column-name-embedding, value) pair rather than a fixed feature slot. This single move (a) gives an open-vocabulary tokenizer that ingests arbitrary heterogeneous columns including high-cardinality strings with zero per-column lookup tables, (b) makes the SAME tokenizer reusable across tables with different/renamed columns, enabling cross-table pretraining and transfer, and (c) handles missing values by dropping nodes. For 70+ heterogeneous features, the reusable recipe is: LM-embed every column name once, represent numerical cells as scalar*column-name-vector and categorical/string cells as LM(string), and let attention modulate column importance via the edge feature.
limitations: Authors-stated / observed: (1) numerical representation is admittedly weak (future work cites adopting PLR/periodic embeddings of Gorishniy 2022); on numeric-heavy tables TabPFN beats it. (2) Only ONE attention layer at fine-tune time (over-smoothing risk), limiting multi-hop interaction. (3) High compute cost that GROWS with train size (n=512: ~315s vs CatBoost ~1s) — 'calls for further optimizations'. (4) No evaluation beyond ~2048 train samples and no test at large column counts (70+); pretraining graphlets center on ~15 relations. (5) Transfer requires the source and target to have RELATED OUTCOMES y (it moment-matches y via power transform and binarization); loosely chosen sources may not help, hence the ensemble fallback. (6) No temporal/time-varying support.
pitfalls: 1) 'Tabular foundation model' here does NOT yet mean wide-table or large-n: all results are small-sample (<=2048) and roughly <=15-relation graphlets; do not assume it works at 70+ columns with many rows without re-testing (topic scaling anti-pattern). 2) The headline win is concentrated on STRING/high-cardinality datasets that the authors deliberately curated (Table 3) — on standard numeric benchmarks the advantage shrinks and TabPFN can win, so the comparison is benchmark-composition-sensitive (proposal's GBDT-vs-deep contradiction caveat applies). 3) Numerical tokenization is just scalar*column-name-vector with a power transform — far simpler than PLR/periodic embeddings in this topic's numerical-embeddings route; its strength is from the string LM + pretraining, not from numeric handling. 4) Transfer/multi-table gains depend on having source tables with related targets and on the bagging+softmax ensemble; a single pairwise learner can underperform single-table. 5) FastText is a static, English-leaning embedding (no contextual LM); semantic matching of entities/columns is only as good as FastText's vocabulary.
relevance_to_topic: Provides the open-vocabulary, language-model-on-column-names tokenizer that turns heterogeneous mixed numerical/categorical/string columns into a permutation-invariant graph with native missing-value handling and zero per-column engineering — directly relevant to tokenizing 70+ heterogeneous features and to cross-table transfer — but it is validated only at small scale (<=2048 rows, ~15 columns) and has a weak numerical encoder, so it is a transferable-idea source, not a drop-in solution at the topic's feature/row scale.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/soda-inria/carte (pip install carte-ai); scikit-learn-style CARTERegressor / CARTEClassifier, pretrained weights released. Uses FastText (cc.en.300) for string embedding.
